Handle: Reciprocal square roots between 2026 and 2027
Estimated MOHS: 8M-12M
IMO slot guess: P1/4
Topic tags: Alg/estimation; telescoping; integral comparison
Core ideas:
Compare 1/sqrt(k) against differences like 2(sqrt(k+1) - sqrt(k))
Use a telescoping or integral squeeze for the partial sum
Narrow N numerically with endpoint control
Rationale (1-2 lines): This is a clean estimate problem with very low search cost. The only real task is picking the right comparison and handling the final localization carefully.
Common pitfalls: Using only the crude estimate sum about 2sqrt(N), which is not sharp enough by itself; losing track of endpoint errors.
Confidence: High
Notes (optional): This is much closer to olympiad prelim / AIME-style estimation than to full IMO invention.
Handle: Perpendicular bisector, circle through A,C,D, and DE || AB
Estimated MOHS: 28M-30M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Geo - Euclidean; circle geometry; coordinates / power-of-a-point
Core ideas:
Put BC on a line and parameterize D on BC using the perpendicular-bisector condition
Write E on the line through D parallel to AB
Use the circle through A,C,D to solve for the parameter of E
Finish with a distance-squared identity
Rationale (1-2 lines): Once coordinates are chosen well, the proof is quite tractable. The hardness comes from deciding that a coordinate attack is the right simplification, since the synthetic target is not immediately transparent.
Common pitfalls: Trying to force a pure angle chase; not exploiting DE || AB early enough; choosing messy coordinates.
Confidence: High
Notes (optional): A very polished synthetic proof may exist, but the coordinate route keeps the problem in the upper P1 / lower P2 zone.
Handle: Roceña sequence with polynomial upper bound
Estimated MOHS: 40M-42M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: NT/recurrences; discrete dynamics; gcd structure
Core ideas:
Analyze local patterns forced by the recurrence
Detect the key rigid block behavior, especially repeated consecutive values
Show the block regenerates at larger scale
Deduce exponential growth, contradicting the polynomial bound
Rationale (1-2 lines): Very deceptive statement. The hard part is discovering the right dynamical invariant/pattern; once found, the recurrence becomes rigid and the growth contradiction is fairly decisive.
Common pitfalls: Searching for a constructive tame sequence for too long; checking examples without extracting the eventual structural pattern.
Confidence: Medium-High
Notes (optional): This is exactly the kind of short problem that can be badly underrated if one looks only at execution length.
Handle: If a(n) is a cube, then so is a(n+2520)
Estimated MOHS: 23M-25M
IMO slot guess: P1/4
Topic tags: NT - lcm divisibility; prime powers; Catalan/Mihailescu
Core ideas:
Interpret a(n) via L(k) = lcm(1,2,...,k)
Note that a(n) can only equal k at jump points of L(k), namely when k+1 is a prime power
Classify perfect cubes among such k using consecutive perfect powers
Check the surviving cases under adding 2520
Rationale (1-2 lines): Nice number-theoretic packaging, but the intended insight is compact and clean. Once the jump-point viewpoint is found, the problem collapses quickly.
Common pitfalls: Treating a(n) as a vague divisibility statistic instead of an lcm-threshold; missing that most k can never occur as a(n).
Confidence: High
Notes (optional): Elegant rather than deep.
Handle: Hotel Las Rozas three-dish scheduling count
Estimated MOHS: 34M-36M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Comb - recurrence; state compression; permutations
Core ideas:
Encode each guest by a permutation of the three dishes across their three dinners
Translate overlapping-stay conditions into local window constraints
Normalize dish labels and reduce to a very small state machine / transfer recurrence
Count valid extensions and unwind the initial choices
Rationale (1-2 lines): The execution is not fearsome once the right encoding is found, but turning the story into the correct finite-state recurrence is the real hurdle.
Common pitfalls: Counting evening-by-evening greedily; not compressing states; overcounting equivalent dish relabelings.
Confidence: Medium-High
Notes (optional): This is the kind of combinatorics problem that feels impossible until the state model is chosen correctly.
Handle: Incircle, contact chord EF, and orthocenter of ADG
Estimated MOHS: 33M-35M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Geo - incircle; pole-polar; analytic geometry
Core ideas:
Model the incircle as a unit circle with BC as a tangent
Recognize EF as the polar of A
Use AG || BC to locate G very cleanly in that model
Compute the orthocenter and show it lands back on the circle
Rationale (1-2 lines): The statement looks synthetic, but the hidden pole-polar viewpoint makes it much shorter. The main burden is spotting that viewpoint or choosing the right incircle coordinates.
Common pitfalls: Heavy trigonometric bash; forgetting that EF is the contact chord/polar of A; chasing orthocenter angles directly.
Confidence: Medium-High
Notes (optional): If one sees the polar setup quickly, this drops a lot; otherwise it feels much nastier.
Handle: Coloring coefficients of product over k = 0 to n
Estimated MOHS: 26M-28M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Alg/comb; coefficients; inclusion-exclusion; support sets
Core ideas:
Observe the polynomial depends only on x1 + x2 + ... + xn
Color monomials by their nonempty support
Evaluate the polynomial on 0-1 vectors to get zero sums on support-downsets
Use inclusion-exclusion / Möbius inversion to isolate each exact support class
Rationale (1-2 lines): This is a very “aha” problem. The execution is short once support-coloring is guessed, but that guess is not standard unless you are comfortable with coefficient-sum extraction tricks.
Common pitfalls: Trying to pair coefficients ad hoc; not exploiting evaluations on 0-1 inputs.
Confidence: High
Notes (optional): Beautiful problem, but it depends heavily on seeing the right combinatorial encoding.
Handle: Floor-sum parity inequality for a prime p
Estimated MOHS: 42M-45M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: NT - floor sums; modular counting; Beatty/Sturmian flavor
Core ideas:
Rewrite the parity using floor(ak/p) - floor((a-1)k/p), which is a 0/1 quantity
Interpret negative terms as a crossing event in a modular/lattice picture
Prove these events are sufficiently balanced on initial segments 1 to b
Convert that balance into the required nonnegative partial sum
Rationale (1-2 lines): The statement is compact, but the correct reformulation is highly non-obvious. This has the feel of a serious olympiad number theory problem where the main idea is everything.
Common pitfalls: Blind floor manipulation without extracting the 0/1 indicator; trying small cases and guessing a pattern without a counting model.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): If there is an unexpectedly slick involution or geometric interpretation, the rating could drop a bit, but on first principles this feels hard.
Handle: Unit-area rectangle with 0 or more than 2025 points
Estimated MOHS: 45M-48M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: Comb/geo; extremal geometry; transversal/stabbing flavor
Core ideas:
Exploit affine freedom in choosing rectangles of fixed area
Rule out the possibility that every such rectangle always has between 1 and 2025 interior points
Use a global packing/averaging or transversal contradiction
Force either an empty rectangle or unbounded multiplicity behavior
Rationale (1-2 lines): This is a global geometric existence statement with very little local traction. The search space is huge, and the intended argument is likely conceptual rather than computational.
Common pitfalls: Restricting mentally to axis-aligned rectangles; reasoning only from finite grids or density heuristics.
Confidence: Medium-Low
Notes (optional): Strong Danzer-type / geometric transversal flavor. High invention cost.
Handle: Red lattice points labeled by nearest blue in row or column
Estimated MOHS: 38M-40M
IMO slot guess: P2/5
Topic tags: Comb - extremal lattice; double counting; level sets
Core ideas:
Guess the sharp exponent from square-block examples
Express the total label sum through level sets {label at least t}
Relate large labels to long red runs in both a row and a column
Use double counting / compression to prove the optimal exponent
Rationale (1-2 lines): Identifying the right exponent is not too bad, but proving it sharply is another matter. This feels like an extremal combinatorics problem with a nontrivial structural inequality.
Common pitfalls: Overfocusing on one-dimensional runs; proving only a quadratic bound; missing the sharp example that suggests the correct exponent.
Confidence: Medium
Notes (optional): The exact exponent is likely the heart; once that is guessed, the proof still has real bite.
Handle: Tangents of circles ABM and ACM, radical-axis target
Estimated MOHS: 46M-50M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: Geo - tangents; radical axis; homothety; advanced synthetic geometry
Core ideas:
Decode the two common external tangents into a homothety/radical-axis framework
Relate the constructed circle to the circumcircle via equal powers
Use the angle bisector and perpendicular bisector conditions as hidden metric constraints
Likely one decisive transformation or symmedian-type observation unlocks everything
Rationale (1-2 lines): The configuration is extremely baroque and the target point is far removed from the data. Even strong geometers will spend a long time just deciding what the picture is trying to say.
Common pitfalls: Pure angle chase with no structural transformation; getting lost in tangent-circle bookkeeping.
Confidence: Medium-Low
Notes (optional): This is the most likely “true P3/P6 killer” in the set.
Handle: No infinite arithmetic progression of chaotic integers
Estimated MOHS: 42M-46M
IMO slot guess: P3/6
Topic tags: NT - additive forms; congruences; arithmetic progressions
Core ideas:
Search for residue obstructions to the form a^3 + b^3 + abc
Combine those obstructions with the structure of an infinite arithmetic progression
Use a modulus-selection / CRT strategy or equivalent local-global contradiction
Rule out the possibility that one residue class can be entirely chaotic
Rationale (1-2 lines): The form looks elementary, but the obstruction is not obvious at all. This feels like a genuine invention problem unless one happens to spot the right congruence structure very early.
Common pitfalls: Arguing from density alone; checking only small moduli; assuming sum-of-cubes intuition is enough.
Confidence: Low
Notes (optional): If the intended proof uses a particularly sharp modular idea, the true rating could sit a bit lower or a bit higher than this band.
